skill.name: flux-2-klein
skill.description: Generate images with Flux 2 Klein (Black Forest Labs' distilled fast variant of Flux 2) on RunComfy — bundled with the model's documented prompting patterns so the skill gets sharper output than naive prompting against the same model. Documents Flux 2 Klein's strengths (sub-second latency, multi-reference brand styling, declarative subject-first prompts), the step-count strategy (4–8 for fast iteration, ~25 for polish), the 9B vs 4B variant trade-off, and when to route to Flux 2 Pro / Seedream 5 / GPT Image 2 instead. Calls `runcomfy run blackforestlabs/flux-2-klein/9b/text-to-image` (or `/4b/`) through the local RunComfy CLI. Triggers on "flux 2 klein", "flux-2-klein", "flux klein", "BFL flux 2", or any explicit ask to generate with this model.
skill.summary: Fast image generation with Flux 2 Klein on RunComfy CLI with optimized prompting patterns
skill.author: runcomfy-com

security_audit.summary: All 83 static analysis findings are false positives. This is a legitimate image generation skill documentation file. The flagged patterns are: (1) Markdown inline code formatting detected as shell commands, (2) Benign service documentation URLs, (3) Legitimate config file paths, and (4) Scanner misidentifying resolution specs (4K/8K) and model sizes (4B/9B) as cryptographic algorithms. No actual security risks identified.

high_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detection
high_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 'weak cryptographic algorithm' at lines 4, 63, 122, 124, 140, 152, 160, 185, 189, 191. These are FALSE POSITIVES. The scanner misidentified resolution specs like '4K', '8K' and model size variants '4B', '9B' as cryptographic algorithm references. No cryptographic weaknesses exist in this skill documentation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms the flagged text contains resolution specs (4K) and model sizes (4B/9B) - not cryptographic algorithm references

high_findings.title: False Positive: External Command Execution
high_findings.description: 54 patterns flagged as 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' are FALSE POSITIVES. These are markdown inline code formatting using backtick syntax for CLI command examples like `runcomfy run ...`. The skill is documentation only, not executable code. Commands are shown as examples, not executed.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill file contains only markdown documentation. The backtick patterns are code formatting in markdown, not shell command execution

high_findings.title: False Positive: Path Traversal Sequence
high_findings.description: High-risk 'path traversal sequence' flagged at lines 208-210 are FALSE POSITIVES. These lines describe the download whitelist domain patterns (.runcomfy.net, .runcomfy.com) for the RunComfy CLI's download feature, not filesystem path traversal.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Lines 208-210 document the CLI's whitelist of download domains, not actual path traversal patterns in code

high_findings.title: False Positive: Hidden File Access
high_findings.description: 'Hidden file in home directory' and 'Hidden file access' flagged at line 219 are FALSE POSITIVES. Line 219 documents the legitimate RunComfy CLI config file location (~/.config/runcomfy/token.json) with proper security notes about mode 0600 permissions.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file path ~.config/runcomfy/token.json is standard Unix configuration file documentation, not hidden file exploitation

high_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance
high_findings.description: Low-risk 'system reconnaissance' flagged at lines 33, 45, 115, 151 are FALSE POSITIVES. These are markdown table formatting characters (|---|) and table content, not system enumeration commands.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged lines contain markdown table syntax, not system reconnaissance commands

high_findings.title: False Positive: At Command Scheduling
high_findings.description: 'at command scheduling' flagged at line 65 is a FALSE POSITIVE. The scanner misidentified the word 'at' as the Unix at-command. Line 65 contains the word 'at' in the phrase 'at 16:9' referring to aspect ratio.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Context shows '16:9' aspect ratio cap, not the Unix at command for task scheduling

low_findings.title: Benign: Hardcoded URLs to RunComfy Service
low_findings.description: 8 'hardcoded URL' detections are legitimate documentation. URLs point to runcomfy.com, docs.runcomfy.com, and model-api.runcomfy.net - the actual service endpoints for this skill. No malicious domains detected.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs are legitimate RunComfy service endpoints documented for user reference

content.user_title: Generate Images with Flux 2 Klein on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Need fast AI image generation with sub-second feedback? Flux 2 Klein delivers rapid creative iteration on RunComfy CLI with optimized prompting patterns for professional results.
actual_capabilities: Generate images with Flux 2 Klein 4B variant in sub-second latency for rapid iteration
Generate high-quality images with Flux 2 Klein 9B variant for polished final output
Apply multi-reference style transfer with up to 4 reference images for brand-consistent assets
Execute conditional edits by specifying what stays and what changes in reference images
Download generated images to specified output directories with automatic URL handling
limitations: Maximum resolution ceiling of approximately 2K — route to Seedream 5 for higher resolution
Aspect ratio capped at 16:9 — extreme wide or tall ratios get cropped
Prompt length limited to approximately 512 tokens — longer prompts degrade quality
Text rendering less precise than GPT Image 2 for embedded text and multilingual signage

use_cases.title: Live Art Direction for Creative Teams
use_cases.description: Iterate rapidly on visual concepts during client sessions with sub-second feedback from the 4B variant. Generate multiple style variations quickly to explore directions without long waits.
use_cases.target_user: Creative directors, designers, and marketing teams

use_cases.title: Rapid Product Visualization
use_cases.description: Create fast product mockups and UI previews during development sprints. Use multi-reference alignment to maintain brand consistency across product image sets.
use_cases.target_user: Product managers, UX designers, e-commerce teams

use_cases.title: AI Art Workflow Integration
use_cases.description: Build Flux 2 Klein into automated pipelines for concept art generation, mood board creation, and visual research. Use the 9B variant with ~25 steps for final polish.
use_cases.target_user: AI artists, game developers, content creators

prompt_templates.title: Basic Product Photo
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a product photo of [product description] on [background] with [lighting style]
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick product image generation with specified attributes

prompt_templates.title: Subject-First Declarative Scene
prompt_templates.scenario: Follows Flux 2 Klein's trained grammar structure for optimal results

prompt_templates.title: Conditional Edit with Reference
prompt_templates.prompt: Same [aspect to preserve] as the reference image, but change [element to modify] to [new value]. Keep [elements to maintain] exactly as shown.
prompt_templates.scenario: Reference-guided editing with explicit preservation instructions

prompt_templates.title: Brand-Consistent Multi-Reference
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the visual style from reference images to create [subject description]. Ensure consistent [style elements] across all references while generating [new subject].
prompt_templates.scenario: Multi-reference brand styling for unified asset generation

output_examples.input: A sleek wireless headphone resting on a marble surface, soft studio lighting from the left, shallow depth of field, 50mm lens, white background, premium product photography
output_examples.output: A high-quality product photograph matching the specified lighting and composition with clean white background suitable for e-commerce

output_examples.input: Same composition and lighting as the reference, but change the background from beach to mountain studio with soft gray backdrop
output_examples.output: The product retains exact same position, shadow, and lighting while background transforms to specified studio setting

best_practices: Use subject-first declarative grammar: front-load the main subject, then add action, scene, style, lighting, camera, and quality modifiers in that order
Choose the 4B variant for rapid iteration and concepting (4-8 steps), then switch to the 9B variant for final polish (~25 steps) using the same prompt grammar
Keep reference images in the same visual register (all photorealistic or all illustrative) to maintain consistent style guidance
anti_patterns: Avoid conflicting adjectives that cancel each other out — 'minimalist + ornate' produces degraded results
Do not request 4K resolution or ultra-wide aspect ratios beyond 16:9 — the model caps at ~2K and crops extreme ratios
Avoid prompts exceeding ~512 tokens — the model degrades in quality rather than truncating gracefully

faq.question: When should I use Flux 2 Klein 4B versus 9B?
faq.answer: Use the 4B variant for rapid concepting and live iteration where sub-second feedback matters. Use the 9B variant for final polished output where quality matters more than speed.

faq.question: How do reference images work with Flux 2 Klein?
faq.answer: Pass up to 4 reference images for style transfer and guided composition. Keep all references in the same visual register (all photos or all illustrations) for best results.

faq.question: What happens if my prompt is longer than 512 tokens?
faq.answer: The model degrades in output quality rather than truncating gracefully. Keep prompts concise and focused on key elements for best results.

faq.question: Can I generate images with text or logos?
faq.answer: Flux 2 Klein handles basic text rendering with its 8B Qwen3 embedder, but GPT Image 2 is more precise for embedded text, logos, and multilingual signage.

faq.question: What output formats does the RunComfy CLI produce?
faq.answer: The CLI downloads generated images as files to your specified output directory. It polls the API every 2 seconds until completion, then fetches results automatically.

faq.question: How do I cancel an image generation mid-process?
faq.answer: Press Ctrl-C while polling to send a cancellation request to the API endpoint, stopping billing for GPU time you no longer need.
